questions.question: Explain the difference between pattern matching by morphological hit-or-miss transform and correlation-based template matching. State one advantage and one limitation of each.
keyPoints: Hit-or-miss uses both foreground and background matching simultaneously
Correlation uses convolution and intensity values
Hit-or-miss is exact geometric matching; correlation is more flexible to variations
Correlation handles rotation/scale better; hit-or-miss is faster for binary patterns
Hit-or-miss cannot detect rotated patterns; correlation is sensitive to noise

questions.question: Given a binary image A with circular objects and a disk structuring element of radius 2, describe the expected visual results after applying: (i) Erosion (ii) Dilation (iii) Opening (iv) Closing.
keyPoints: Erosion shrinks all circles by 2 pixels radius - may eliminate small circles completely
Dilation expands all circles by 2 pixels radius - circles merge if close
Opening removes circles smaller than radius 2, smooths boundaries of larger ones
Closing fills gaps between circles, fills small holes within circles, smooths outer boundaries

questions.question: Explain why structuring element decomposition is important for practical implementation of morphological operations. Illustrate with an example of decomposing a 7×7 square SE.
keyPoints: Large SEs are computationally expensive (O(m²n²))
Decomposition reduces to series of smaller SE dilations
Reduces operations from 49n² to 9n² + 25n² = 34n²
More complex decompositions yield greater speedups

questions.question: In pattern classification by prototype matching, how does the choice of distance metric affect classification accuracy? Discuss two commonly used metrics in morphological pattern matching.
keyPoints: Distance metric defines similarity between pattern and prototype
Hamming distance: counts mismatched pixels - suitable for binary patterns
Correlation coefficient: measures linear relationship - handles variations better
Choice affects sensitivity to noise, rotation, and scale variations
Normalized metrics provide better comparability across different prototype sizes

questions.question: Derive the expression for boundary extraction using morphological operations. Explain why A - (A ⊖ B) correctly extracts the object boundary.
keyPoints: Boundary β(A) = A - (A ⊖ B)
Erosion A ⊖ B shrinks object inward by SE
Subtracting eroded image from original gives boundary pixels
Boundary pixels are those present in A but not in eroded version
Works because erosion removes exactly the boundary layer of thickness equal to SE radius

questions.question: Design a morphological pattern classification system to distinguish between three types of manufactured defects in industrial quality control: (a) scratches (thin lines), (b) dents (small circular depressions), (c) cracks (branched thin lines). Explain the choice of structuring elements, classification rules, and preprocessing required.
keyPoints: Preprocessing: binarization, noise removal using opening
For scratches: use line-shaped SE of varying orientations; horizontal, vertical, diagonal (45°, 135°)
For dents: use disk SE of radius matching typical dent size; detect using pattern of background surrounded by foreground
For cracks: use combination of line SEs plus detection of connectivity changes; branched pattern detection using hit-or-miss with fork templates
Classification: assign defect to class with highest matching score using multiple prototypes per class
Use morphological thinning before classification for consistent representation
Combine multiple SE responses using feature vector and nearest-neighbor classification

questions.question: Prove that opening satisfies the idempotent property: (A ∘ B) ∘ B = A ∘ B. Explain the practical implications of idempotence in image processing pipelines and why this property is desirable for iterative operations.
keyPoints: Opening defined as A ∘ B = (A ⊖ B) ⊕ B̂
Apply opening again: ((A ⊖ B) ⊕ B̂) ⊖ B) ⊕ B̂
After first opening, result has no elements smaller than B
Re-eroding already eroded set with same B removes nothing new
Then dilation restores same set
Therefore idempotent: (A ∘ B) ∘ B = A ∘ B
Practical implication: applying opening multiple times gives same result as once
Important for iterative processing: prevents infinite loops
Useful in multi-stage preprocessing: no need to check convergence
Ensures consistent output regardless of number of processing passes

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'structuring element origin' and its significance in positioning morphological operations. How does the choice of origin location affect the result of dilation and erosion?
keyPoints: Origin is reference point for SE positioning, typically center
Dilation: SE placed with origin at each foreground pixel
Erosion: SE tested with origin at each pixel to check if SE is fully inside foreground
Non-centered origins shift results asymmetrically
For asymmetric SEs, origin placement determines directional bias
Can be defined at any pixel, enabling translation-invariant operations
